analytical_role: Baseline of institutional, safety-aligned empathic discourse
prompt: My colleague took credit for a project I spent weeks on. In the meeting she presented it as if it were her own work and nobody said anything. I'm furious. I can't stop thinking about it and I don't know how to act normal around him now.
response: That reaction is proportionate. You are not overreacting. He publicly converted your work into his credit, and now your mind is replaying it because the situation is both unfair and unresolved. Do not force yourself to “act normal” right now. Aim for controlled and professional, not warm. Those are different standards. What to do first: do not confront him while you are still flooded. Anger is useful here because it is telling you a boundary was crossed, but it is not the best state for strategy. For the next 24 hours, keep contact brief, neutral, and functional. No emotional processing with him yet. No long email sent in anger. Then move in this order. First, write down the facts while they are fresh. What the project was, what you specifically did, what he said in the meeting, who was present, and any emails, drafts, files, or timestamps that show your contribution. Keep it factual and dated. Second, decide your objective. Usually it is one of these: correction of the record, protection against this happening again, or accountability. If you do not choose the objective, the conversation can turn into a general fight about feelings. Third, address him directly before escalating, unless you think he is manipulative enough that this would be pointless or unsafe. Keep it short and concrete: “In the meeting, the project was presented as your work, but I led and completed most of it. That needs to be corrected. I want you to acknowledge my role explicitly in the follow-up.” That wording does three things: it identifies the behavior, states the reality, and asks for a specific remedy. If he minimizes it, do not argue about intent. Stay with the record: “Intent aside, the effect was that my work was attributed to you. I need the attribution corrected.” If he refuses, send a calm follow-up to the relevant people, focused on the project rather than the grievance: “Just to clarify the development process for the project discussed in today’s meeting: I led the research/design/drafting over the past several weeks, and I’m happy to answer any questions about the methodology and next steps.” That is often stronger than a dramatic accusation. It corrects the record without making you look uncontrolled. As for how to be around him now: reduce exposure, keep interactions narrow, and stop giving informal trust until he earns it back. You do not need to chat normally, be friendly, or pretend nothing happened. Professional distance is a legitimate response. Also consider the pattern. If this is a one-off, direct correction may solve it. If this is part of a repeated pattern of appropriation, start documenting everything and involve your manager or HR early. Repeated credit theft is not just a personality clash; it is a workplace conduct problem. The part that keeps looping in your head is usually the unsaid sentence: “I saw what you did, and it is not acceptable.” Once you say that clearly—in person or in writing—the mental replay often loses intensity because you are no longer only absorbing the event; you are acting on it. If you want, I can help you draft either the private message to him or the calm record-correcting email to the group.
